qa_pairs.question: What is the output of the `phello2.c` program when run with 4 processes using `mpirun`?
qa_pairs.answer:
When `phello2.c` is run with `mpirun -np 4 ./phello2`, the output will be similar to:
`[P_0] process 3 said: "Hello, world! from process 3 of 4"`
`[P_1] process 0 said: "Hello, world! from process 0 of 4"`
`[P_2] process 1 said: "Hello, world! from process 1 of 4"`
`[P_3] process 2 said: "Hello, world! from process 2 of 4"`

qa_pairs.question: How does the MPI standard define `MPI_Send` and `MPI_Recv` calls?
qa_pairs.answer: The MPI standard defines `MPI_Send` and `MPI_Recv` as blocking calls.

qa_pairs.question: When does an `MPI_Send` call return according to the MPI standard?
qa_pairs.answer: `MPI_Send` will not return until it is safe for the calling module to modify the contents of the provided message buffer.

qa_pairs.question: When does an `MPI_Recv` call return according to the MPI standard?
qa_pairs.answer: `MPI_Recv` will not return until the entire contents of the message are available in the message buffer the caller provides.

qa_pairs.question: How does the presence of an MPI library buffer affect `MPI_Recv` operations?
qa_pairs.answer: Whether or not the MPI library provides buffering does not affect receive operations. `MPI_Recv` will not return until the data is received and placed directly in the message buffer, meaning the call remains blocked until then.

qa_pairs.question: How does the presence of an MPI library buffer affect `MPI_Send` operations?
qa_pairs.answer: If the MPI library provides an internal buffer, `MPI_Send` need not block. Once the message is copied out of the original data location into the buffer, it is safe for the user to modify that location, so the call can return.

qa_pairs.question: Why did the parallel "Hello, world!" program (e.g., `phello2.c`) not deadlock when all processes called `MPI_Send` first?
qa_pairs.answer: The program didn't deadlock because the MPI library likely provided internal buffering. This allowed `MPI_Send` calls to return even before the corresponding `MPI_Recv` was ready, as the message data was copied into the buffer.

qa_pairs.question: What is meant by an "unsafe" MPI program?
qa_pairs.answer: An "unsafe" MPI program is one whose correctness relies on a buffered implementation of `MPI_Send`, which is not a requirement of the MPI standard.

qa_pairs.question: What is considered a "safe" MPI program?
qa_pairs.answer: A "safe" MPI program is one that does not rely on a buffered implementation in order to function correctly.

qa_pairs.question: Provide an example of pseudo-code that illustrates an MPI deadlock.
qa_pairs.answer:
An MPI deadlock can be illustrated by the following pseudo-code fragment:
`if (rank == 0) { MPI_Recv(from 1); MPI_Send(to 1); } else if (rank == 1) { MPI_Recv(from 0); MPI_Send(to 0); }`

qa_pairs.question: Why does the given deadlock pseudo-code fragment lead to a deadlock?
qa_pairs.answer: The processes in this MPI application will block on the receive calls and deadlock because receives are executed on both processes before their matching send, regardless of whether buffering is present.

qa_pairs.question: Provide an example of pseudo-code that illustrates an "unsafe" MPI program.
qa_pairs.answer:
An "unsafe" MPI program can be illustrated by the following pseudo-code fragment:
`if (rank == 0) { MPI_Send(to 1); MPI_Recv(from 1); } else if (rank == 1) { MPI_Send(to 0); MPI_Recv(from 0); }`

qa_pairs.question: Why is the "unsafe" MPI pseudo-code fragment considered unsafe, and when might it deadlock?
qa_pairs.answer: This code is unsafe because it relies on buffering. It may work if buffering is provided by the library, but if the library is unbuffered, or if messages are large enough to fill the buffer, this code will block on the sends and deadlock.

qa_pairs.question: Provide an example of pseudo-code that illustrates a "safe" MPI program.
qa_pairs.answer:
A "safe" MPI program can be illustrated by the following pseudo-code fragment:
`if (rank == 0) { MPI_Send(to 1); MPI_Recv(from 1); } else if (rank == 1) { MPI_Recv(from 0); MPI_Send(to 0); }` (Note: This refers to the corrected pattern where one side sends first, the other receives first).

qa_pairs.question: Why is the "safe" MPI pseudo-code fragment guaranteed not to deadlock?
qa_pairs.answer: Even in the absence of buffering, the send here is paired with a corresponding receive between processes. While a process may block for a time until the corresponding call is made, it cannot deadlock.

qa_pairs.question: What is a common strategy to rewrite the "Hello, World!" program to make its communication safe?
qa_pairs.answer: A common solution is to adopt an odd-even pairing and perform the communication in two steps.

qa_pairs.question: How does the odd-even pairing strategy work to ensure safe communication in the "Hello, World!" program?
qa_pairs.answer: In the odd-even pairing strategy, all even-ranked processes execute a send followed by a receive, while all odd-ranked processes execute a receive followed by a send. This ensures that sends and receives are properly paired, avoiding any possibility of deadlock.

qa_pairs.question: Does the odd-even pairing strategy prevent deadlock if the number of processors is odd?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, the odd-even pairing strategy still prevents deadlock even if the number of processors is odd. For instance, process 0 (even) sends to process 1 (odd), which receives first. When process 0's send completes, it then receives from process N-1 (even), whose send eventually completes. There might be a small delay, but no deadlock.
